title: Benchmark parser ignores normal Go benchmem output

evidence.quote:
print(f"\n{'=' * 60}✅ No performance regressions detected")
sys.exit(0)

reasoning: The regex expects integer ns/op and decimal B/op, then casts the B/op capture to int. Standard Go -benchmem output commonly has decimal ns/op and integer B/op, for example 3.5 ns/op and 2 B/op. Those lines are skipped entirely, leaving empty result dictionaries; compare_benchmarks then reports no regressions and exits 0, so the regression check can silently pass without comparing anything.
recommendation: Update the parser to accept both integer and decimal numeric fields using the actual Go benchmark format, parse B/op consistently, and make comparison fail when no benchmarks or no common benchmark names are parsed.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No linked tests are included, and there is no fixture asserting that real go test -bench -benchmem output is parsed.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add fixtures with standard Go benchmark lines using decimal ns/op and integer B/op, then assert parse_benchmark_file returns metrics and compare_benchmarks exits nonzero for a slower current result.
